title: Caches are little time machines
hook: A cache makes yesterday feel instant. That is wonderful until yesterday is wrong and you cannot figure out which layer is still living there.

beats.heading: A cache trades freshness for speed

blocks.html: Every cache is a bet: this answer will still be good later. Browsers bet. CDNs bet. APIs bet. Databases bet. Your app probably bets several times before the user sees a pixel. Most of the time, that is why everything feels fast.

blocks.title: reliability control room
blocks.caption: Flip the switches and watch the story turn into a tiny operating model.
blocks.low: brittle: the system works only while the weather is perfect
blocks.mid: steadier: one failure can happen without a full mystery
blocks.high: resilient: pressure, waiting, and recovery are all visible

items.label: common layers: browser, CDN, app, database
bad invalidation can make the whole stack look haunted

blocks.caption: Systems stories get more interesting when you can feel the pressure move through the stack.

blocks.html: The server sends a response with caching instructions. Cache-Control can say how long a response is fresh, whether a shared cache may store it, and whether the client must revalidate before reuse. ETag gives the client a cheap way to ask, "is my copy still good?"

blocks.code:
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Cache-Control: public, max-age=300, stale-while-revalidate=60
ETag: "profile-v42"

{ "name": "Mo", "plan": "pro" }
blocks.caption: Five minutes fresh, one minute stale while the cache quietly checks again.

beats.heading: Stale data is a time-travel bug

blocks.html: The painful cache bugs are not crashes. They are contradictions. The admin sees the new plan. The user sees the old plan. Support sees both depending on which tab they opened first. That is not one bug, it is a map of who is reading from which time.

blocks.title: zsh · chasing stale data
blocks.caption: Follow the age before changing code.

lines.t: # user is seeing a five-minute-old answer

blocks.html: Some data can be old for a while. Logos, docs, marketing pages, and public assets love long caches. Account state, payments, permissions, and safety decisions need a tighter story. The right question is not "can we cache this?" It is "how wrong can this be, and for how long?"
